title: Comparable vs Comparator
description: Defining custom sort order for objects -- finally explaining how TreeSet/TreeMap actually decide 'sorted order' for something more complex than a String or int.
objectives: Understand Comparable as a class's own, single natural ordering
Understand Comparator as an external, swappable ordering

sections.text: In the Set and Map lessons, TreeSet and TreeMap sorted Strings alphabetically and numbers numerically, without explanation of HOW Java knew that order. For your own classes, like Student, Java has no idea what 'sorted' should even mean -- by name? By age? Comparable and Comparator let you define this.
2. Comparable: A Class's Own Natural Order
An interface implemented by a class itself to define its single, default (natural) ordering, via a compareTo(other) method.

sections.title: Reading compareTo's return value
rows: this and other are considered equal for sorting purposes

sections.text: this.age - other.age is a common shorthand: if this.age is smaller, the result is negative (comes first); if larger, positive (comes after); if equal, zero. Collections.sort() (from java.util.Collections) uses this compareTo() automatically for any Comparable class.
3. The Limitation: Only ONE Natural Order

Comparable is written INSIDE the class itself, so a class can only have ONE compareTo() -- one fixed 'natural' order. But what if you sometimes want Students sorted by age, and other times by name? Comparable alone can't do both. That's exactly what Comparator solves.
A separate object defining a specific ordering for a class, passed into a sort call, allowing multiple different sort orders for the same class without modifying it.

a.name.compareTo(b.name) uses String's OWN compareTo (String implements Comparable<String> itself, sorting alphabetically). Notice Student here doesn't implement anything at all -- the sorting logic lives entirely in the separate byName object instead.

sections.code:
Comparator<Student> byAge = (a, b) -> a.age - b.age;
Collections.sort(students, byAge);

sections.text: (a, b) -> a.age - b.age is a lambda expression -- a shorter way to write the exact same Comparator without the verbose new Comparator<Student>() { ... } syntax from the previous example. We only preview this shorthand here; the full lambda syntax is beyond this course's current scope, but recognizing it in code you read is valuable.
6. Comparable vs Comparator, Side by Side

sections.title: Comparable vs Comparator
rows: As many as you want to define

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to give a class multiple natural orderings via Comparable -- it only supports one; use separate Comparator objects for alternate orderings.
Forgetting compareTo/compare must return negative/zero/positive consistently -- returning only true/false-like values (like 1 and -1 exclusively, ignoring 0) can break sorting for equal elements.
Confusing Comparable's compareTo(other) (one argument, called on an instance) with Comparator's compare(a, b) (two arguments, called on a separate object).
Forgetting Collections.sort(list) alone requires the elements to implement Comparable -- calling it on a plain class with no Comparable and no Comparator argument fails to compile.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?
questions.answer: Comparable is implemented by the class itself to define its single natural ordering via compareTo(other). Comparator is a separate object defining a specific ordering via compare(a, b), letting the same class be sorted in multiple different ways without modifying the class.

questions.question: What does a negative, zero, or positive return value from compareTo mean?
questions.answer: Negative means the current object comes before the other; zero means they're equal for sorting purposes; positive means the current object comes after the other.

questions.question: If you want to sort a List of Student objects by age in one place and by name in another, which should you use?
questions.answer: Comparator -- since Comparable only supports one natural ordering per class, having multiple different sort criteria requires separate Comparator objects, one per ordering needed.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Comparable is implemented by a class itself to define its one natural ordering, via compareTo(other) -- negative means before, zero means equal, positive means after. Comparator is a separate object with a compare(a, b) method, letting the same class be sorted in multiple different ways (like byName or byAge) without modifying the class or being limited to one ordering. Collections.sort(list) uses a class's Comparable ordering directly; Collections.sort(list, comparator) uses a specific Comparator instead. This is exactly what powers TreeSet and TreeMap's automatic sorting for custom objects, once those objects implement Comparable or a Comparator is supplied.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Comparable = the class's OWN built-in ruler, one ordering only. Comparator = a SEPARATE, swappable ruler you bring from outside -- as many rulers as you need, for as many orderings as you want.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What's the key difference between Comparable and Comparator?
What does a negative return value from compareTo mean?
If you need two different sort orders for the same class, which should you use?
What method does Comparable require? What method does Comparator require?
